---
title: "Programming with LLMs"
subtitle: "Programming with LLMs for Data Practitioners"
author: "Julia Silge | Posit PBC"
footer: "<https://juliasilge.github.io/applied-stats-byu-2026/>"
highlight-style: atom-one
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    transition: fade
    preview-links: auto
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: "../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg"
      data-background-size: "auto 45%"
      data-background-position: "82% 85%"
      data-background-repeat: "no-repeat"
---

# Programming with LLMs {background-image="../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg" background-size="auto 45%" background-position="82% 85%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

A deeper look at what you can do with LLMs from code that you can't easily do in a chat UI.

# Choosing a model {background-image="../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg" background-size="auto 45%" background-position="82% 85%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}
## The landscape (15 min)

-   Major providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, plus local options like ollama
-   Tradeoffs: capability, context length, speed, cost
-   Newer is not always better for your task

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Open `05-models` and send the same prompt to several providers:

-   Change one string to switch providers: `chat_openai()` vs. `chat_anthropic()`, etc.
-   Compare answers, latency, and token usage.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=10 >}}

# Multi-modal input {background-image="../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg" background-size="auto 45%" background-position="82% 85%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}
## Beyond text (15 min)

-   Vision: pass an image alongside your prompt
-   PDFs: pass document bytes directly
-   When to extract text up front vs. let the model see the file

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Try a multi-modal input:

-   Open `06-vision` and pass food photos to ask for recipe suggestions.
-   Open `07-pdf` and take a PDF recipe and turn it into clean Markdown.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=10 >}}

# Structured output {background-image="../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg" background-size="auto 45%" background-position="82% 85%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}
## Getting parseable answers (15 min)

-   R: `ellmer::type_*()`
-   Python: Pydantic models with `chatlas`
-   Schemas double as documentation for the model

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Open `08-structured-output` and extract structured fields from the recipe PDF (ingredients, steps, yield, prep time).

Tip: turn on `use_attribute_docstrings` in Pydantic to let docstrings guide the model.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=15 >}}

# Parallel and batch calls {background-image="../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg" background-size="auto 45%" background-position="82% 85%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}
## Scaling out (10 min)

-   When one prompt becomes a hundred
-   Parallel API calls vs. provider batch APIs (cheaper, slower)
-   Watch for rate limits

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Open `09-batch` and re-run the structured extraction across many recipes in parallel. Save the JSON output so we can reuse it later.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=10 >}}

# Prompt engineering and hallucinations {background-image="../images/undraw_large-language-models_m4no.svg" background-size="auto 45%" background-position="82% 85%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}
## Models will confidently make things up (35 min)

-   What hallucination looks like
-   Why a confident answer is not a correct answer

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Open `10-plot-interpret` and ask the model about a plot:

-   Generate a plot of mpg vs. weight from `mtcars`.
-   Ask the model to interpret it.
-   Discuss what it got right and where it leaned on priors.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=10 >}}

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Open `11-plot-noise`:

-   Replace the plot with random noise; keep the prompt the same.
-   See what the model says.
-   Part 2: pair up and try to steer the model into a "decent" interpretation of pure noise.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=15 >}}

## System prompt best practices

-   Be explicit about role, audience, and format
-   Show, don't just tell (few-shot examples)
-   Tell the model how to handle uncertainty

## Your turn

::: {.callout-note icon=false}

## Activity

Open `12-quiz-game-prompt` and write the system prompt for our quiz game show.
:::

{{< countdown minutes=15 >}}


# Thanks!

<center>

<img src="https://github.com/juliasilge.png" style="border-radius: 50%;" width="300px"/>

[{{< fa brands github >}} \@juliasilge](https://github.com/juliasilge)

[{{< fa brands bluesky >}} \@juliasilge.com](https://bsky.app/profile/juliasilge.com)

[{{< fa link >}} juliasilge.com](https://juliasilge.com/)

</center>

::: footer
Illustrations from [unDraw](https://undraw.co/)
:::
